title: मन्त्रपुष्पम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री मन्त्रपुष्पम् ॥

योपां पुष्पं वेद पुष्पवान् प्रजावान् पशुमान् भवति।
चन्द्रमा वा अपां पुष्पम् पुष्पवान् प्रजावान् पशुमान् भवति।
य एवं वेद योपामायतनं वेद आयतनवान् भवति॥ १॥

अग्निर्वा अपामायतनम् आयतनवान् भवति।
यो अग्नेरायतनं वेद आयतनवान् भवति।
आपो वा अग्नेरायतनम् आयतनवान् भवति।
य एवं वेद योपामायतनं वेद आयतनवान् भवति॥ २॥

वायुर्वा अपामायतनम् आयतनवान् भवति।
यो वायोरायतनं वेद आयतनवान् भवति।
आपो वै वायोरायतनम् आयतनवान् भवति।
य एवं वेद योपामायतनं वेद आयतनवान् भवति॥ ३॥

असौ वै तपन्नपामायतनम् आयतनवान् भवति।
योमुष्यतपत आयतनं वेद आयतनवान् भवति।
आपो वा अमुष्यतपत आयतनम् आयतनवान् भवति।
य एवं वेद योपामायतनं वेद आयतनवान् भवति॥ ४॥

राजाधिराजाय प्रसह्यसाहिने नमो वयं वैश्रवणाय कुर्महे।
स मे कामान् कामकामाय मह्यं कामेश्वरो वैश्रवणो ददातु।
कुबेराय वैश्रवणाय महाराजाय नमः॥ ५॥

॥ इति श्री मन्त्रपुष्पम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
benefits: Sacred Mantra Pushpam for devotion and spiritual growth
Invokes blessings of Vedic in all forms
Traditionally recited for removal of sins and bestows spiritual merit
Ideal for Vedic festivals and daily worship
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Vishnu, Mantra Pushpam (मन्त्रपुष्पम्) stands as a sacred prayer. Rooted in Taittiriya Aranyaka, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Regular recitation is believed to invoke blessings of Vedic in all forms, remove sins and bestow spiritual merit, and grant divine protection and auspiciousness. It is especially recommended on Sunday (Ravivar), Monday (Somvar), Tuesday (Mangalvar), Wednesday (Budhvar), Thursday (Guruvar), Friday (Shukravar), and Saturday (Shanivar) and during Diwali and Ganesh Chaturthi for maximum spiritual benefit. At 5 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 4 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Mantra Pushpam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Mantra Pushpam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Mantra Pushpam (मन्त्रपुष्पम् — flower of mantras) is one of the most sacred Vedic hymns in Hindu liturgy, traditionally recited as the final mantra of every Vedic puja, abhisheka and homa — symbolically offering the puja itself as a flower (pushpam) to the deity. The text is drawn from the Taittiriya Aranyaka of the Krishna Yajurveda (Prashna 1, Anuvaka 22) and consists of 5 mantra-paragraphs in the distinctive vakya-yajus prose style. Its central revelation is the secret-knowledge (rahasya-jnana) that water (apah) is the cosmic ayatana (foundation, abode, support) of all the elements — fire, wind, sun — and whoever knows this becomes ayatanavan (foundationally-established). The hymn culminates in salutation to Vaishravana-Kubera, lord of wealth. The Mantra Pushpam is mandatorily recited at the end of every Hindu puja across India. (Source: Taittiriya Aranyaka — Krishna Yajurveda.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is the Mantra Pushpam recited at the end of every puja?
seoFaqs.answer: The Mantra Pushpam holds a unique position as the universal puja-conclusion mantra for several reasons: (1) Its very name pushpam (flower) symbolizes the ritual offering — the entire puja is offered to the deity as a single fragrant flower-mantra; (2) It is a mantra-rahasya (secret-knowledge) declaring the cosmic interrelation of waters, fire, wind, sun and moon — making it a complete cosmological invocation; (3) Its phala-shruti — pushpavan prajavan pashuman bhavati and ayatanavan bhavati — promises the worshipper full possession of flowers (beauty), progeny, cattle (wealth) and a firm foundation in life; (4) The closing salutation to Kubera-Vaishravana (Rajadhirajaya prasahyasahine namo vayam Vaishravanaya kurmahe) invokes wealth and prosperity at puja-completion; (5) The act of standing with anjali-folded-hands while reciting this mantra (known as Mantra Pushpanjali) is itself the final pushpa-anjali (handful of flowers) offering. Every Vedic priest concludes every puja with this mantra. (Sources: Taittiriya Aranyaka; Vedic puja paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Yopam Pushpam Veda?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening line — Yopam pushpam veda pushpavan prajavan pashuman bhavati, chandrama va apam pushpam pushpavan prajavan pashuman bhavati, ya evam veda yopam-ayatanam veda ayatanavan bhavati — translates as: Whoever knows the flower (pushpam) of the waters (apah) becomes possessed of flowers (pushpavan), progeny (prajavan), and cattle (pashuman). The moon (chandrama) is verily the flower of the waters; whoever knows this becomes possessed of flowers, progeny and cattle. Whoever knows the foundation (ayatanam) of the waters becomes ayatanavan (firmly-established). The verse establishes the secret-knowledge (rahasya) chain: waters → moon (their flower / essence); moon → waters (the moon's water-substance); fire → waters (its ayatana); wind → waters; sun → waters. This Vedic cosmology recognizes water as the universal element-mother, with the moon being water's flowering-essence, sun being water's source through evaporation, and fire-wind being water-foundation-dependent. (Source: Mantra Pushpam verse 1, Taittiriya Aranyaka.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the connection of Mantra Pushpam with Kubera and prosperity?
seoFaqs.answer: The closing mantra (verse 5) — Rajadhirajaya prasahyasahine namo vayam Vaishravanaya kurmahe, sa me kaman kamakamaya mahyam Kameshvaro Vaishravano dadatu, Kuberaya Vaishravanaya Maharajaya namah — translates as: We offer salutation to the king-of-kings (Rajadhiraja), the irresistible-conqueror (Prasahya-sahin), Vaishravana (son of sage Vishrava — that is, Kubera). May He, Kameshvara (Lord of all desires), Vaishravana, grant me — desire-bearer (kamakama) — the fulfillment of my desires (kaman). Salutations to Maharaja Kubera Vaishravana. This concluding salutation invokes Kubera, the lokapala of the northern direction and the cosmic treasurer who grants material prosperity. By concluding every puja with this Kubera-salutation, the Mantra Pushpam ensures that both the spiritual fruit (from the puja) and the material wealth (from Kubera) are secured for the worshipper. This is why Mantra Pushpam is especially central to Lakshmi Puja, Diwali Puja, Akshaya Tritiya and Dhanteras. (Sources: Mantra Pushpam verse 5; Kubera upasana tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should the Mantra Pushpam be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Mantra Pushpam is universally recited as the concluding mantra of every Vedic puja — daily, weekly and festival-based. It is especially central on: Diwali / Deepavali (the supreme prosperity festival, recited during Lakshmi Puja and Kubera Puja), Dhanteras (the day before Diwali — Kubera's special day), Akshaya Tritiya (Vaishakha Shukla Tritiya — the gold-buying day), Ganesh Chaturthi (every day of the 10-day Ganesh Utsav), Navaratri (during Devi puja conclusion), every Sankashti and Vinayaka Chaturthi, every Pradosha (Shiva puja), at all temple abhishekas and homas, at every gruha-pravesh (housewarming) ritual, and at every Vedic samskara (sixteen sacraments from birth to death). The proper protocol is to stand (or sit if elderly), join palms in anjali-mudra, and recite the entire Mantra Pushpam as the final pushpa-anjali offering — earning the name Mantra Pushpanjali in Marathi and Maharashtrian tradition. (Sources: Vedic puja paddhati; pan-Indian temple ritual.)

seoFaqs.question: How does the Mantra Pushpam differ from the Vedic Pushpanjali offering?
seoFaqs.answer: Both relate to flower-offering at puja-conclusion but differ in form. (1) The physical Pushpanjali (पुष्पाञ्जलि — handful-of-flowers offering) is the actual ritual act of offering loose flowers to the deity at puja-conclusion, accompanied by the recitation of a sankalpa-shloka and the namaskara mantra appropriate to that particular deity (Lakshmi, Ganesha, Devi, Vishnu etc.). (2) The Mantra Pushpam is a specific Vedic hymn from the Taittiriya Aranyaka recited at the same puja-conclusion stage that is itself called the flower-mantra — it functions as the mantra-pushpa-anjali whether or not physical flowers are also being offered. In contemporary practice (especially in Maharashtra, Karnataka and South India) the term Mantra Pushpanjali combines both — the standing ritual where the worshipper recites Mantra Pushpam while holding flowers, and offers them at the conclusion. The combined act is the universal Hindu puja-completion ritual. (Sources: Vedic puja tradition; Maharashtrian-South Indian ritual practice.)
